coredata.dc:description: A ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on dual-emission carbon quantum dots (CQD) was developed to real time monitor food spoilage. Two hydrophobic electrospun fluorescent films were developed using polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) as the film-forming polymer in combination with CQD as the fluorescent probe. The CQD/PVDF film and CQD@PVDF film enabled the analysis of TMA with limits of detection (LODs) of 1.04 μM and 2.1 μM, respectively, and they exhibited excellent stability at 4 °C. By these virtues, the CQD@PVDF film exhibited visible fluorescence color changes from yellow green to blue by real time and nondestructively sensing volatile amines generated from beef, pork and shrimp in a packaging system with high humidity. This strategy provided a simple but useful, non-destructive, robust, and platform to real time monitor food spoilage for intelligent food packaging.

head.ce:title: A ratiometric fluorescence amine sensor based on carbon quantum dot-loaded electrospun polyvinylidene fluoride film for visual monitoring of food freshness

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on dual-emission carbon quantum dots (CQD) was developed to real time monitor food spoilage. Two hydrophobic electrospun fluorescent films were developed using polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) as the film-forming polymer in combination with CQD as the fluorescent probe. The CQD/PVDF film and CQD@PVDF film enabled the analysis of TMA with limits of detection (LODs) of 1.04 μM and 2.1 μM, respectively, and they exhibited excellent stability at 4 °C. By these virtues, the CQD@PVDF film exhibited visible fluorescence color changes from yellow green to blue by real time and nondestructively sensing volatile amines generated from beef, pork and shrimp in a packaging system with high humidity. This strategy provided a simple but useful, non-destructive, robust, and platform to real time monitor food spoilage for intelligent food packaging.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Meats and aquatic foods, such as livestock, poultry, fish and shrimps, are important food sources because they are rich in proteins, fats, minerals and so on. Cold storage (0–4 °C) is one of the common preservation methods used for fresh meats and aquatic foods, since it maintains the original organoleptic properties and retains nutritional components for an extended period exceeding non-refrigerated counterparts. However, despite cold storage conditions, the freshness of these foods generally can decrease rapidly due to microbial contamination, such as Pseudomonas and Bacillus (Odeyemi, Alegbeleye, Strateva, & Stratev, 2020). To ensure food quality and safety, it is important to evaluate the real time freshness of meats and aquatic foods under cold storage in the supply chain (Zhai, Shi, Zou, Wang, Jiang, Zhang, et al., 2017).

Traditional methods to assess food quality are primarily based on physicochemical and microbiological analysis. However, these methods are usually time-consuming and destructive to food samples. Therefore, they are difficult to be practically applied to monitor the freshness of meats and aquatic foods in real time. To address this problem, some rapid and non-destructive methods have also been developed, including near-infrared spectroscopy, hyperspectral imaging, and e-nose systems (Kutsanedzie, Guo, & Chen, 2019). These methods have been proven to be very effective at evaluating real time quality of meats and aquatic foods. Unfortunately, these methods require expensive equipment and highly trained technicians, which inevitably leads to a high cost for food manufacturers and suppliers.

Currently, increasing attention has been paid to portable sensors in a variety of areas, because they are generally simple, economical, and convenient to use. In recent years, various advanced optical sensors (Lin, Wu, Li, Song, Guo, & Fu, 2016; Zhai, Li, Zhang, Shi, Zou, Huang, et al., 2018; Zia, Mancini, Bustreo, Zych, Donno, Athanassiou, et al., 2021) and electrical sensors (Ma, Chen, Cheng, Yan, Pan, Shi, et al., 2018; Singh, Singh, Kaur, & Singh, 2022; Kim, Park, Park, Kim, Seo, An, et al., 2022) have shown good potential to achieve in situ and real time monitoring of food freshness. Compared with electrical sensors, optical sensors exhibit some advantages, including power-free devices, highly visible color changes and generally lower costs. To date, most optical sensors for monitoring the freshness of meats and aquatic foods are based on their sensitivity to volatile amines. This is due to volatile amine gases, such as trimethylamine, dimethylamine, and ammonia, being largely generated during protein degradation by microbes, and consequently, these volatile amines are regarded as important freshness indicators (Siripongpreda, Siralertmukul, & Rodthongkum, 2020; Zhai, Zou, Shi, Huang, Sun, Li, et al., 2020).

Fluorescent sensors generally have high sensitivities and good stabilities. Recently, some amine-responsive fluorescent sensors have also been developed for monitoring the freshness of aquatic foods (Zhang, Wang, Xu, Shi, & Yang, 2022). Nevertheless, most reported fluorescence sensors rely on the changes of single fluorescence emission intensities, making their color changes difficult to observe with the naked eye (Jia, He, Yang, Sun, Bu, Zhuang, et al., 2023). Ratiometric fluorescence sensors can effectively overcome this visual limitation. Ratiometric fluorescence sensors not only exhibit diverse color changes, but also have self-calibration ability to reduce interference from the external environment (Han, Yang, Luo, He, Yu, Li, et al., 2019). For example, a cellulose-based ratiometric amine-responsive fluorescent sensor was developed using fluorescein isothiocyanate and protoporphyrin IX (Quan, He, Zhou, Liang, Wang, Tian, et al., 2021). The sensor presented excellent sensitivity to biogenic amines and was accompanied with fluorescent color change from red to yellow green.

Carbon quantum dots (CQD) are carbon nanomaterials with sizes less than 10 nm. CQD have received great interest due to their low toxicity, low cost, high stability, highly fluorescence nature and biocompatibility (Rasheed, 2023). CQD has also been used to develop aquatic food freshness sensors. For example, a pH-responsive red single-emission CQD was synthesized and a filter paper was used as a loaded substrate to produce a fluorescent label that could monitor shrimp spoilage (Zhang, Chen, Peng, Zhou, Zhuang, Zhang, et al., 2020). This utilized N, S-doped single-emission CQD with high selectivity for ammonia. The CQD was used directly in the form of solution to indicate fish freshness (Zhang, Xu, Shi, & Yang, 2021). Apart from single-emission CQD, dual-emission CQD was synthesized as a fluorescent probe for volatile amines and successfully produced a fluorescent label that could monitor seafood freshness by using cotton paper as a loaded substrate (Zhang, Sun, Ma, Qin, & Tang, 2023). These studies indicated that CQD would be suitable candidates to develop food freshness sensors.

In this study, we used a one-step hydrothermal method to synthesize a dual-emission CQD with high amine sensitivity as a ratiometric fluorescent probe. To make a portable hydrophobic sensor for monitoring food freshness in highly humid environments, the synthesized CQD was loaded to electrospun polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) film through two independent ways. One was depositing CQD on the surface of PVDF film, defined as CQD/PVDF film. The other was embedding CQD into PVDF film, defined as CQD@PVDF film. The microstructure, stability, and gas sensitivity of these two films were investigated and compared. Finally, their performance in monitoring the freshness of typical meats and aquatic foods was assessed. Compared with reported studies, our sensors have several advantages: (1) Compared with ratiometric sensors constructed by using two fluorescent substances, we used a single dual-emission CQD to directly constitute a ratiometric sensor. This fluorescent probe was easier to prepare and more stable to resist interference from other factors. (2) Compared with most micron-scale paper-based sensors, we used the electrostatic spinning method to load CQD for the first time. The electrospun nanoscale fibers made the sensors more sensitive to gases. (3) Compared with most hydrophilic paper-based sensors, we used hydrophobic polymers to load the fluorescent probe, namely CQD, which endowed the sensor with better stability in fresh food packaging environment with high humidity.

Fresh beef (tenderloin), pork (tenderloin), shrimp (Metapenaeus ensis) and salmon (Oncorhynchus keta) were purchased from Gemmellon supermarket markets (Zhenjiang, China). p-phenylene diamine (p-PD) was purchased from Aladdin Co., Ltd. Sulfuric acid (H2SO4, 98 %), disodium hydrogen phosphate (Na2HPO4), sodium dihydrogen phosphate (NaH2PO4), hydrochloric acid (HCl), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), NNdimethylformamide (DMF), ethanol and trimethylamine (TMA·H2O, 25–27 %) were all purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) was purchased from Millipore Co., Ltd. Ultrapure water was used throughout the whole work. All reagents were analytical reagents without further purification before use.

Firstly, 0.216 g of p-PD was dissolved in 20 mL C2H5OH, and 120 μL of sulfuric acid was added to the above p-PD solution. Then, the mixed solution was transferred into a 30 mL Teflon reactor and heated at 200 °C in an oven for 3 h (Wang, Yang, Zhang, Ge, Zhang, Wang et al., 2019). The solution was poured into a 50 mL centrifuge tube and centrifuged at 12000 rpm for 15 min to remove the non-fluorescent precipitates. The obtained solution was then purified by silica gel column chromatography using a mixture of ethanol and ethyl acetate (the volume ratio was 1:1) as eluent to remove the unreacted material. The above steps were repeated three times to obtain the purified solution. Finally, the sulfur-doped CQD was obtained after vacuum drying in an oven, and the CQD was prepared into 1.0 mg/mL solution with ethanol.

First, a blank PVDF film was prepared using an electrostatic spinning machine (ET-2535 M, Yongkang, China). Specific implementation methods referred to previous literature with a slight modification (Ahmed, Lalia, & Hashaikeh, 2015; Zaarour, Zhu, Huang, & Jin, 2018). The DMF and acetone dual-solvent system was chosen for better solubilization of PVDF. 1.5 g of PVDF powder was dissolved in 10 mL of a double-solvent system (the volume ratio of DMF and acetone was 4:1) at 40 °C for 3 h. The obtained PVDF solution was defoamed by ultrasonic (KQ-100KDE, Kun Shan Ultrasonic Instruments, China) for 35 min. Then, 10 mL of the PVDF solution was slowly inhaled into 10 mL disposable syringes using metal needles (inner diameter of 0.61 mm). Silicone paper was covered on the metal receiver to collect the electrospun fibers. The voltage was set to 18 kV and the distance between the syringe and the receiver was adjusted to 20 cm. At the same time, the relative humidity and temperature were set to 50 % and 35 °C, respectively. Afterwards, the flow rate of the PVDF solution was set to 0.6 mL/h and the receiving roller was controlled to run at 50 r/min. The exhaust system was opened to remove volatile organic solvent gas. At the end of the electrospinning, the obtained PVDF film was placed in a 40 °C oven for 1 h to remove residual organic solvents. The film was cut into small square pieces with a side length of 1 cm. The CQD/PVDF film was prepared by dripping 2.5 μL of CQD solution (0.1 mg/mL) onto the surface of the blank PVDF film. Then, the CQD/PVDF film was further dried in an oven at 40 °C. Finally, the CQD/PVDF film was placed in a silica gel desiccator for further use.

Firstly, 1.5 g of PVDF powder was dissolved in 10 mL of a double-solvent system (the volume ratio of DMF and acetone was 4:1) at 40 °C for 3 h. After that, 1 mL of CQD solution (1.0 mg/mL) was slowly added to the above PVDF solution and magnetically stirred for 1 h to obtain CQD-PVDF mixture. Then, the prepared solution was defoamed by ultrasonic for 35 min. Finally, CQD-PVDF mixture was subsequently treated to obtain CQD@PVDF film using electrospinning method, in the same manner as the above PVDF solution to obtain PVDF film. The mass unit area of CQD in the CQD@PVDF film could be derived from the mass of added CQD and the spinning area of the whole CQD@PVDF film. The unit area mass of CQD in the CQD/PVDF film could be obtained from the volume of CQD added dropwise and the area of CQD distribution. It is worth pointing out that by using the above methods, the CQD weights per unit area of the CQD/PVDF film and CQD@PVDF film were the same.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Response of CQD solution to TMA
ce:section.ce:para: Different concentrations and the same volume of TMA solution were added to 3 mL of CQD solution to prepare a series of solutions. The final concentration of CQD solution was 0.3 mg/mL. After mixing for three minutes, the fluorescence spectra of the mixed solution were measured using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (F98, Prismatic Technology, China). Excitation wavelength was 400 nm; emission wavelength range was 420–700 nm; scanning speed was 1000 nm/min; excitation and emission bandwidth were 10 nm. Parallel experiments were performed three times and the average was taken as the final result.

The films were placed in incubators at temperatures of 4 and 25 °C. The fluorescent photos of the films were taken in a UV box using a digital camera (EOS 1500D, Canon, Japan). The L* (lightness), a* (red-to-green), and b* (blue-to-yellow) values of the photos were collected using the image processing software developed by our group. Parallel experiments were performed three times and the average was taken as the final result. The total color difference (ΔE) was calculated as Eq
(1) :
(1)
Δ E =
( Δ L ∗ )
2
+
( Δ a ∗ )
2
+
( Δ b ∗ )
2
where ΔL* = L* − L0*, Δa = a* − a0*, Δb = b* − b0*. L*, a* and b* are the color parameters of films after a certain storage time. L0*, a0* and b0* are the initial color parameters of films.

TMA was considered to be a representative gas of meat spoilage (Yang, Zhang, & Jia, 2023). The film strips (1 cm × 1 cm) were placed on top of a testing chamber with a constant temperature (25 °C) and relative humidity (75 %). The solution of TMA was injected into the testing chamber using a microliter syringe and left to stand for 1 h. The concentrations of TMA in the chamber were 0, 5.75, 11.5, 23, 34.5, 57.5, 80.5, 126.5, 172.5 and 207 μM. Parallel experiments were performed three times and the average was taken as the final result. The concentration of TMA in the chamber was calculated as Eq
(2) :
(2)
C
=
W
S
V S
ρ S
MV
where C (mol/L) is the concentration of the substance to be tested in the testing chamber; ρ s is the density of the substance to be tested (g/mL); V s is the volume of the liquid substance to be measured (mL); W s is the mass fraction of analyte in the solution; M is the molar mass of the substance to be tested (g/mol); V is the volume of the reaction vessel (L). The images of the films in the UV box were recorded with the camera, and L*, a* and b* values were extracted. A picture of the UV box for taking photo was shown in Fig. S4 with a description of the structure.

Since fresh meat packaging has a high humidity environment, the effect of humidity on the CQD/PVDF and CQD@PVDF film sensing ability was also explored. The humidity chambers with different RH were first prepared. The actual RH in the desiccators was determined to be 30 % (MgCl2 saturated solution), 60 % (NaBr saturated solution), and 90 % (ZnSO4 saturated solution), by using a commercial hygrometer. The films were adhered to the top of the humidity chamber for half an hour of equilibration and then a drop of TMA solution was added to the inner wall of the humidity chamber to obtain a final gas concentration of 250 μM. The fluorescent color change of the films was recorded in a UV box and analyzed.

90 g of fresh beef (tenderloin), pork (tenderloin), shrimp and salmon were placed in transparent boxes whose tops were attached two types of films and placed in a refrigerator at 4 °C. The fluorescence color changes of the film were observed in a UV box and recorded by a camera every 24 h for 7 days. In the meantime, the TVB-N values of beef, pork, shrimp and salmon were measured according to GB 5009.228–2016. To make sure these two set of samples were the same as far as possible, we used the same big piece of pork, beef, salmon and the same batch of shrimp. At the first day, the big piece of pork, beef or salmon sample was diced into small pieces with uniform size and divided the shrimp into smaller portions of equal mass. Some of pork, beef, salmon and shrimp were put into packaging device with CQD/PVDF and CQD@PVDF film, and other pork, beef, salmon and shrimp were put into the same packaging device without CQD/PVDF and CQD@PVDF film. They were stored under the same condition. The samples used for TVB-N test were taken from the packaging device without CQD/PVDF and CQD@PVDF film. Briefly, 10 g of sample was placed in 75 mL of distilled water and they were broken into homogenates with a homogenizer. The mixture was filtered to obtain a filtrate. Afterwards, the filtrate was transferred to a distillation tube containing 1 g of MgO. 30 mL of boric acid (20 g/L) was used as the receiving solution. The automatic acid-base titrator (Hanon Instruments, T860, China) titrated boric acid solutions with 0.01 M HCl solution. The TVB-N content was calculated based on the amount of 0.01 M HCl consumed during the titration process and was expressed in mg/100 g. Parallel experiments were performed three times and the average was taken as the final result.

The morphology and particle size of the as-prepared CQD were investigated using transmission electron microscopy (TEM). CQD powder was dispresed in acetone before drop-casted onto carbon-coated copper grids for TEM measurements. As shown in Fig. 1
A, the CQD presented a spherical morphology and the average particle size of CQD was around 7.85 nm. The HRTEM image exhibited a clear lattice structure with a pitch of 0.21 nm which was similar to the (100) facet of graphitic carbon (Fig. 1 B) (Si, Zhang, He, Yang, Yan, Liu, et al., 2018). In addition, the quantum yield (QY) of CQD was measured with quinine sulfate as the standard material (excitation at 360 nm, QY = 54 %) was 31 %.

Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum was obtained to determine groups of CQD. As shown in Fig. 1 C, three characteristic bands at 1103, 1041 and 994 cm−1 were related to the stretching vibration of OSO, due to the presence of the -SO3H group on CQD (Travlou, Secor, & Bandosz, 2017). The absorption peak at 3416 cm−1 corresponded to the OH stretching vibration. Two absorption peaks at 914 and 862 cm−1 were attributed to the CH vibration of the benzene ring bent outward. The absorption peak at 1616 cm−1 belonged to the CC stretching of benzene ring. The absorption peak at 571 cm−1 was the bending vibration of the CS bond and 1381 cm−1 was the characteristic stretching band of the CN bond. These results indicated that sulfur was successfully doped in CQD.

Fig. 1 D shows the X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of CQD. A broad diffraction peak around 2θ = 28° was observed which corresponded to the (002) plane with an interlayer spacing of 0.318 nm (Kumari, Kumar, Sahu, & Kumar, 2018).

The Ultraviolet–Visible (UV–Vis) and fluorescence spectra were recorded to study the optical properties. As shown in Fig. 1 E, the absorption peak at 260 nm was attributed to the π-π* transition of the conjugated CC bond. The weak peak at 340 nm was usually attributed to the n-π* transition of CO bonds of CQD (Shi, Su, Zhang, Liu, Huang, & Zhao, 2016). The absorption band near 350–400 nm was due to the n-π* transition of the CO bond (Li, Kang, Liu, & Lee, 2012). The UV–Vis absorbance curve of the as-prepared CQD showed a broad absorption range that could be attributed to the complex energy band structure and random energy levels of CQD (Ding, Cheng, Ma, Kong, & Xiong, 2013).The insets of Fig. 1 E show that the CQD solution presented orange-yellow color in daylight and yellow color under a 365 nm UV lamp. Fig. 1 F shows the fluorescence emission spectra of CQD at different excitation wavelengths (360–460 nm). The CQD displayed two maximum emission peaks at 495 and 550 nm under an excitation wavelength of 400 nm. With the increase of excitation wavelength, the emission peak did not produce a large red shift. This fluorescent behavior might be related to the narrow size distribution and surface passivation of CQD (Somaraj, Mathew, Abraham, Ambady, Mohan, & Mathew, 2022).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Response of CQD solution to TMA
ce:section.ce:para:
The fluorescence spectra of dual-emission CQD in response to different concentrations of TMA solution were determined. As shown in Fig. 2
A, the emission intensity at 495 nm increased while the emission intensity at 550 nm decreased, with the increase of TMA concentration. Accordingly, the color of the CQD solution changed from yellow to slightly red under daylight and from yellow to cyan under UV light (365 nm) (Fig. 2 A insets). According to the fluorescence spectra, the relationship between the fluorescence intensity ratio (F 495/F 550) and the TMA concentration was determined. As shown in Fig. 2 B, there was a good linear relationship between F 495/F 550 and TMA concentration in the range of 0–240 μM (R 2 = 0.9950). The above results indicated that the dual-emission CQD was suitable to be used as a ratiometric nanoprobe for TMA sensing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Response of CQD solution to pH
ce:section.ce:para: The pH-sensitive behavior of CQDs was studied by adding CQDs to phosphate buffers of different pH and calibrating their pH with small amounts of NaOH (1 mol/L) and HCl (1 mol/L). As shown in Fig. S1 A, as pH values changed from 3.0 to 11.0, the CQD solutions gradually changed from yellow to cyan under 365 nm UV light. With the increase of pH, the fluorescence intensity at 495 nm gradually increased, while the fluorescence intensity at 550 nm gradually decreased and this fluorescence peak showed a slight blue-shift within pH 3–9 (Fig. S1 B). The calibration curve between F 495/F 550 of CQD and pH values showed a good linear relationship in the range of pH 5–7 (R 2 = 0.9765) (Fig. S1 C). To give more details of the relation between F 495/F 550 and pH values within 5–7, the pH value was increased in intervals of 0.2 (Fig. S1 D). Similarly, F 495 increased while F 550 decreased with the increase of pH (Fig. S1 E), but a better linear relationship (R 2 = 0.9850) was obtained between F 495/F 550 and pH (Fig. S1 F), indicating that the synthesized CQD could also be used as an accurate pH indicator in the range of pH 5–7.

The fabrication of the CQD/PVDF film and CQD@PVDF film is shown in Fig. 3
A. The synthesized CQD was loaded on the PVDF film through two independent ways. CQD/PVDF film was prepared by depositing CQD on the surface of the electrospun PVDF film, while CQD@PVDF film was prepared by electrospinning CQD-PVDF mixture. The film morphology was characterized by using a field emission scanning electron microscope (JSM-7800 M, JEOL, Japan). As shown in Fig. 3 B-D, all films were constituted with rough fibers. Compared with the blank PVDF film with an average fiber diameter of 669 nm, the CQD/PVDF film did not show a significant change in fiber diameter (670 nm) due to the direct addition of CQD to the surface of the blank PVDF film, as shown in Fig. 3 B and C. However, the CQD@PVDF film showed a larger fiber diameter (∼780 nm) than the blank PVDF film and CQD/PVDF film (Fig. 3 D). Due to the small size of the CQD, the CQD are embedded into the PVDF fibers during the solvent evaporation process, which broke the dense crystal structure of the PVDF molecules and increases the fiber diameter during the solvent evaporation process.

This was because, during the squirt flow solvent evaporation process of making the CQD and PVDF mixture, CQD was embedded into the PVDF fiber and thus broke the dense crystal structure of PVDF molecules.

Generally, fresh foods containing high water content would lead to the high humidity of the food packaging and this high humidity could lead to the leaching of color agents of the colorimetric sensors. Hence, it was preferable for the colorimetric sensors to have high hydrophobicity (Zhai, Sun, Cen, Wang, Zhang, Yang, et al., 2022). The water contact angle (WCA) was used to determine the hydrophilicity and hydrophobicity of the prepared films. As shown in Fig. 3 E, the WCA value of the blank PVDF film was 134.24°, indicating its intrinsic hydrophobicity due to the large concentration of C-F groups (Shah, Cho, Nam, Park, Park, & Park, 2020). Compared with the blank PVDF film, the WCA value of the CQD/PVDF film significantly decreased to 117.52°. This was probably because the hydrophilic amino group of CQD adhered to the surface of the blank PVDF film, making it less hydrophobic. However, the WCA value of the CQD@PVDF film was 134.23°, and there was no significant difference between the WCA value of the CQD@PVDF (134.23°) and blank PVDF film (134.24°). This was due to the fact that CQD was mostly embedded into the fibers of PVDF, resulting in a negligible effect on the hydrophobicity of the blank PVDF film. WCA is a measure of the hydrophobicity of a surface, and a hydrophobic surface generally refers to its surface WCA value greater than 90°. Overall, in this study, the CQD/PVDF and CQD@PVDF films both maintained good hydrophobicity.

The fluorescence images of the films under 400 nm excitation were recorded by a laser confocal microscope (TCS SP5, Leica, Germany). As shown in Fig. 3 F, compared with the blank PVDF film, the CQD/PVDF and CQD@PVDF film had good fluorescence luminescence behavior, indicating that CQD was successfully added to the electrospun film. Compared with the CQD/PVDF film, the fiber morphology of the CQD@PVDF film had a few extra nodules and the distribution of CQD in the CQD@PVDF film had improved uniformity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity of fluorescent films to TMA
ce:section.ce:para:
The meat spoilage process can produce a variety of amines, and TMA is one of the most representative volatile amines (Yang, Zhang, & Jia, 2023). Hence, the responses of the CQD/PVDF and CQD@PVDF film to TMA were evaluated. As shown in Fig. 4
A, the fluorescent color of the two films gradually changed from yellow green to blue with the increase of TMA concentration. This indicated that CQD maintained TMA sensitivity although it existed in a non-liquid environment. Surprisingly, this yellow green-to-blue color change of films was much visible than the yellow-to-cyan color change of CQD solution in response to TMA (see Fig. 2 A). This phenomenon probably due to that strong hydrogen bonds force could be occurred between the fluorine atoms of PVDF and the amine hydrogen or hydroxyl hydrogen of CQD. Hydrogen covalently bonded with electronegative atoms, such as oxygen and nitrogen, and then approached the atom fluorine, which has a greater electronegativity. This process produced electrostatic interactions, and formed hydrogen bonds (Taylor, 2017). These hydrogen bonds force changed the surface state of CQD, resulting in an improved fluorescence property of CQD. However, the accurate mechanism of this fluorescence change remains to be further investigated.

The fluorescent color changes of the films were described by using ΔE values. As shown in Fig. 4 B and C, ΔE values increased progressively with the increase of TMA concentration. Linear relationships between ΔE values of the two films and TMA concentration were found in the TMA concentration range of 0–80.5 μM, and the corresponding calibration curve for the CQD/PVDF and CQD@PVDF film was expressed as Eqs (3) and (4).
(3)
y = 0.1527 x + 0.5859 (
R
2
= 0.9736 )
(4)
y = 0.0904 x + 0.3944 (
R
2
= 0.9894 )
where x is the concentration of TMA (μM) and y is the ΔE value.

Accordingly, the limit of detection (LOD) values of the CQD/PVDF and CQD@PVDF film to TMA were determined to be 1.04 μM and 2.10 μM based on Eq (5) (Lu, Chen, Chen, Ma, Wang, Liu, et al., 2023).
(5)
LOD = 3 σ / N
where σ is the standard deviation of blank measurements for 13 repeats, and N is the absolute value of the slope of the calibration curve.

According to the LOD value, it was evident that the CQD/PVDF film was more sensitive to TMA. This was because the CQD in the CQD@PVDF film was mostly embedded in the fiber and could not fully contact with TMA, resulting in a lower sensitivity than the CQD/PVDF film with CQD directly exposed to the surface.

The film after reaction with TMA was placed at room temperature for 12 h to investigate the reversibility of fluorescent color change under UV light (365 nm). It can be observed from Fig. 4 D and E that both the CQD/PVDF and CQD@PVDF films had good reversibility. The initial fluorescent color of CQD/PVDF and CQD@PVDF film was yellow-green. After 6 cycle times, the film could still change from yellow green to blue. This good reversibility was due to the reaction between TMA and CQD being protonation and deprotonation processes. As shown in Fig. 4 F, this proton transfer from the sulfonic acid group of CQD to the nitrogen atom of the TMA molecule is reversible (Zhang, Xu, Shi, & Yang, 2021).

The self-stability of films is crucial to their practical application. Fig. S2 shows the relative fluorescence color changes (ΔE) of the films. It can be seen that the films stored at 4 °C had ΔE values lower than 2 in 14 days, indicating that they had outstanding fluorescent color stability. By contrast, the films had higher ΔE values when they were stored at 25 °C. On the 14th day, the ΔE values for the CQD/PVDF and CQD@PVDF films were 1.72 and 1.11 at 4 °C, respectively. As reported in previous studies, color changes were basically invisible to the naked eye when the ΔE value was less than 2 (Won, Jang, & Jeon, 2016). In this study, the ΔE values were all lower than 2, indicating that the fiber films also had excellent color stability at 4 °C. Due to excessive resonance energy transfer (RET) or direct π-π interactions, the fluorescence of solid-state CQD is always quenching due to aggregation (Yan, Yi, Sun, Zang, Wang, Xu, et al., 2021). Overall, the CQD@PVDF film was more stable than the CQD/PVDF film. The possible reason was that CQD in the CQD@PVDF film was embedded into the fiber, which prevented the CQD from aggregation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of fluorescent films to amine gases
ce:section.ce:para:
During meat and fish spoilage, in addition to amine gases, a variety of other volatile gases, such as aldehydes, ketones, acids, esters, sulfides and alkanes, may also be produced (Pereira, de Sousa Picciani, Calado, & Tonon, 2021). According to the previous studies (Casaburi, Piombino, Nychas, Villani, & Ercolini, 2015; Zhai, Li, Shi, Huang, Sun, Zhang, et al., 2019), some representative components were chosen to investigate the selectivity of films, including ethanol, methanol, acetic acid, ethyl acetate, dichloromethane, cyclohexane, acetone, dimethyl disulfide, dimethylamine and trimethylamine. As shown in Fig. 5
A-C, in the presence of other gases at a concentration of 3 M, the ΔE values of the CQD/PVDF and CQD@PVDF film were less than 2. Under these conditions, the color change of the films could not be observed by the naked eye. When amine gases (dimethylamine, trimethylamine) were present at a concentration of 300 μM, the color of the film changed from yellow-green to blue. The ΔE values of the CQD/PVDF and CQD@PVDF film in the presence of dimethylamine were 18.26 and 16.02, respectively, and the ΔE values of the CQD/PVDF and CQD@PVDF film in the presence of trimethylamine were 29.14 and 21.56, respectively. Fig. 5 D-F show the colors and ΔE of the CQD/PVDF and CQD@PVDF films after the addition of TMA in the presence of these interferences, respectively. This competition experiment clearly revealed that the coexistence of other competing volatile compounds other than acetic acid and dimethyl disulfide did not interfere with the detection of TMA. This could be due to that as CQD had pH-sensitive properties (see Fig. S1), acetic acid decreased the pH of the CQD surrounding environment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of humidity on the sensing ability of films
ce:section.ce:para: The response of the films to TMA at different relative humidity (RH) was investigated. The films were first equilibrated in humidity chambers with different RH for half an hour, then a drop of TMA solution was added on the inner wall of the humidity chamber to obtain a final gas concentration of 250 μM. The CQD/PVDF and CQD@PVDF film changed from yellow green to blue after exposed to the TMA at 30 %, 60 % and 90 % RH Fig. S3A). From Fig. S3 B and C, it could be seen that the color change (ΔE) of the CQD/PVDF or CQD@PVDF film initially increased more quickly under a lower RH. This may be due to the fact that TMA molecules were more easily combined with water vapor under a higher RH environment and so that these TMA molecules could not quickly diffuse to combine with CQD. However, the final ΔE values of the CQD/PVDF film or CQD@PVDF film under different RH were very close. This was probably because those TMA initially combined with water vapor gradually reacted with CQD as water vapor flowed within the reaction chamber or as the dissolved TMA released from water vapor.

Firstly, to investigate the self-stability of films in highly humid food packaging, CQD/PVDF film and CQD@PVDF film were attached to the top of a transparent box containing 10 mL of water. The images of the two films and their ΔE values changed with storage time and are shown in Fig. 6
A. The CQD/PVDF film and CQD@PVDF film had ΔE values of 1.7 and 2.9, respectively on the 7th day, indicating that the CQD/PVDF film was less stable than CQD@PVDF film in a highly humid environment.

The images of the films used to monitor beef, pork, shrimp, and salmon freshness are shown in Fig. 6 B. The colors of the films in beef, pork and shrimp changed from yellow green to blue under 365 nm UV light, while the films in salmon changed to cyan. For both beef, pork, shrimp and salmon, the ΔE of CQD/PVDF film changed more rapidly than that of CQD@PVDF film (Fig. 6 C-F), which was consistent with the results in Fig. 4 that CQD/PVDF had better gas sensitivity than CQD@PVDF film. However, the CQD on the CQD/PVDF film showed visible diffusion phenomenon to some extent, according to the images on the 7th day (Fig. 6 B). This was probably because CQD coated on the PVDF film was largely exposed to water vapor generated from foods, causing the movement of CQD. Unfortunately, this diffusion phenomenon of CQD suppressed the indicating ability of the CQD/PVDF film. By contrast, the CQD@PVDF film was stable enough and no diffusion phenomenon was observed on the 7th day (Fig. 6 B). Hence, in this study, CQD@PVDF film was further used to establish the relation between film color and food freshness.

TVB-N values were used to determine the freshness of meats and aquatic foods. Fig. 6 C shows TVB-N of beef during storage at 4 °C for 7 d, and the corresponding ΔE value of the films. TVB-N values increased from 5.59 to 25.67 mg/100 g with time. According to Chinese standard (GB 2707–2016), the maximum value of TVB-N for beef is 15 mg/100 g. In this study, the TVB-N of beef sample reached 15 mg/100 g at nearly 5.3 d, when the ΔE value of the CQD@PVDF film was 6.25. Hence, the beef sample could not be consumed if the ΔE value of the CQD@PVDF film was higher than 6.25.

Similarly, Fig. 6 D showed that when TVB-N of pork sample reached its limitation 15 mg/100 g (GB 2707-2016), the ΔE value of the CQD@PVDF film was 5.65. Fig. 6 E showed that when TVB-N of shrimp sample reached its limitation 20 mg/100 g (GB 2733-2015), the ΔE value of the CQD@PVDF film was 6.73.
Fig. 6 F showed that TVB-N of salmon reached its limitation 30 mg/100 g (GB 2733-2015), the ΔE value of the CQD@PVDF film was 4.30.

In comparison, salmon showed higher TVB-N content in the limitation time than beef, pork and shrimp, while the ΔE value of CQD@PVDF film used for salmon (ΔE = 4.30) was lower than the ΔE values of CQD@PVDF film used for beef (ΔE = 6.25), pork (ΔE = 5.65) and shrimp (ΔE = 6.73). This was probably because TVB-N represented the total content of volatile basic nitrogen gases, such as TMA, DMA and NH3, while during the spoilage of meats and aquatic foods, other acid gases, such as fatty acids and carbon dioxide, could also be generated. As presented in Fig. 5, acid gases would weaken the color change of CQD in response to basic gases. Since salmon generally has higher fat content than beef (tenderloin), pork (tenderloin) and shrimp, more fatty acids or carbon dioxide might be generated due to the degradation of fats. Hence, the CQD@PVDF film would be more suitable to be used to monitor the freshness of meats and aquatic foods with low fat content.

To better utilize ratiometric fluorescence for real time freshness detection and reduce the error of visual evaluation, an intelligent fluorescence sensing system based on a smartphone was established. By installing the UV light source in a 3D printed box and connecting the power supply, a portable fluorescent light box was successfully composed. The specific process was as follows. As shown in Scheme S1, the TVB-N ∼ ΔE curve was first input into the database. Secondly, the smartphone was placed on the camera hole on the top of the 3D printed box. Then, the fluorescent film images captured by the smartphone were used to extract the L*, a* and b* values of the colors. Then, these data were linked to the TVB-N ∼ ΔE curve to determine the freshness of meats and aquatic foods.

In conclusion, there are several advantages of our fluorescent sensors compared with previously reported sensors, as shown in Table S1. In this study, we synthesized a dual-emission CQD, of which the color change was more obvious for observation for the naked eye than single-emission CQD (Zhang, Xu, Shi, & Yang, 2021). Meanwhile, our dual-emission CQD was obtained by merely one-step heating method, being more convenient to synthesize than the ratiometric sensors with the need of two fluorescent probes (Fu, Wu, Zhou, Zhao, Lan, Huang, et al., 2020; Liu, Xiao, Shang, Zhuang, & Wang, 2022). Finally, we used electrospinning method to embed CQD into hydrophobic polymer PVDF to get a nano-porous film, which was more suitable for practical use than fluorescent solutions (Zhang, Wang, Xu, Shi, & Yang, 2022; J. Zhang, Xu, Shi, & Yang, 2021) and also exhibit better gas sensitivity than those film using cotton paper as the solid matrices (Zhang, Sun, Ma, Qin, & Tang, 2023). Hence, the CQD@PVDF film would have good potential for application in intelligent food packaging.

The dual-emission CQD was one-step synthesized using hydrothermal method and used as a ratiometric fluorescent probe to volatile amines. CQD was loaded to PVDF film through two independent ways to make hydrophobic portable sensors. The CQD/PVDF film and CQD@PVDF film exhibited yellow green-to-blue fluorescence color changes when exposed to TMA, with LODs of TMA of 1.04 μM and 2.1 μM, respectively. Compared with the two films, the CQD/PVDF film had a higher sensitivity, while CQD@PVDF film had a higher stability. In addition, these films showed good selectivity to amines, other than the coexistence of acetic acid due to the pH-sensitive properties of CQD. Experiment results showed that the CQD@PVDF film had better self-stability than CQD/PVDF film during practical use, and it exhibited yellow green-to-blue color changes along with the storage of beef tenderloin, pork tenderloin and shrimp. As the fluorescence color information could be easily observed by naked eye or obtained by using digital measuring techniques, such as a smartphone, the CQD@PVDF film could be used as a low-cost and portable fluorescent TMA sensor for convenient, non-destructive monitoring of meats and aquatic foods freshness in intelligent packaging.
